variables.content:
Assume the true thermodynamic-limit single-quasiparticle band of a 2D HEG is a smooth function \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) in a neighbourhood of the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: fitting the finite-cell discrete DMC band values to the quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4}
      $$
      over a modest interval \\([k_{F}-\\Delta k,\\,k_{F}+\\Delta k]\\) (with \\(\\Delta k\\) chosen so that the interval is not extremely small and contains sufficient discrete \\(k\\)-points to characterise curvature) yields an estimate of the derivative \\(\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}\\) that is an adequately accurate approximation to the true thermodynamic-limit derivative for the purpose of computing the quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}=k_{F}/(d\\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{F}}\\), provided the quartic form captures the local curvature of \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\).

Define the inverse effective-mass tensor at a band extremum k_0 by m_{ij}^{-1}(k_0) = (1/ħ^2) ∂^2E_n(k)/∂k_i∂k_j|_{k_0}, where ħ is the reduced Planck constant and E_n(k) is the band energy for band index n; the proposition is that computing these second derivatives by a finite-difference five-point stencil in reciprocal space with step size Δk=0.01 Bohr^{-1} yields numerical values for the smallest eigenvalue of m_{ij} (reported in units of the electron rest mass m_0) that are accurate to the quoted significant figures (for example, hole m_eff decreasing from 2.011 m_0 at 0%HF to 1.406 m_0 at 50%HF) and that the discretization error from this finite-difference approximation does not materially change the reported near-linear trends or the stated R^2 values for the linear fits.

For DMC-computed discrete single-quasiparticle bands \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) obtained in finite periodic simulation cells for a 2D HEG, define the pathological behaviour as the erratic variation of estimated quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}\\) when the derivative \\(d\\mathcal{E}/dk\\) is estimated by fitting only a very narrow \\(k\\)-range immediately surrounding the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: this near-\\(k_{F}\\) pathological behaviour of \\(m^{*}\\) estimates arises primarily from finite-cell fluctuations (for example, the discrete sampling of \\(\\mathbf{k}\\)-points and cell-induced finite-size correlation effects) together with Hartree–Fock-like non-smooth mean-field features that persist in finite cells, rather than from an intrinsic non-differentiability or singularity of the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle band at \\(k_{F}\\); this explains why expanding the \\(k\\)-range used in the fit reduces the pathology.

The effective-mass tensor components are defined by second derivatives of the band energy at the $\Gamma$ point via $m_{\parallel}^{*-1}=(1/\hbar^2)\partial^2E/\partial k_z^2$ and $m_{\perp}^{*-1}=(1/\hbar^2)\partial^2E/\partial k_x^2=(1/\hbar^2)\partial^2E/\partial k_y^2$, and the masses reported are obtained by fitting HSE06+SOC eigenvalues computed on a $\Gamma$-centered $5\times 5\times 5$ $k$-mesh using finite-difference or curvature-fitting procedures near $\Gamma$. The weak point is the assumption that this numerical procedure produces converged effective-mass values, i.e., that increasing $k$-point density or using denser eigenvalue sampling would not materially change the reported masses.

At each density, spin polarization, and finite system size the discrete DMC band values \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) (where \\(k=|\\mathbf{k}|\\)) were fitted over an extended wavevector interval to a quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4},
      $$
      with fitted coefficients \\(\\alpha_{0},\\alpha_{2},\\alpha_{4}\\). The quasiparticle effective mass was then obtained from the definition
      $$
      m^{*}=\\frac{k_{F}}{\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}},
      $$
      where \\(k_{F}\\) is the Fermi wavevector of the infinite 2D HEG at the given density and spin polarization and the derivative is evaluated at \\(k=k_{F}\\). It was observed that extracting the derivative from fits restricted to a very narrow interval immediately surrounding \\(k_{F}\\) yields ill-behaved (pathological) estimates of \\(m^{*}\\), whereas performing the quartic fit over a larger modest wavevector range around \\(k_{F}\\) produces stable, systematically varying estimates of \\(m^{*}\\). Averaging the band behaviour over such a modest \\(k\\)-range therefore provides a controlled practical procedure to suppress the finite-cell and Hartree–Fock-like near-\\(k_{F}\\) fluctuations that cause the pathological behaviour.

Electron and hole effective masses $m_e^{*}$ and $m_h^{*}$ (reported in units normalized to the free-electron mass $m_0$) are obtained by numerically evaluating the second derivative of the tight-binding band dispersion, $m^{*}=\hbar^2\big/(\partial^2 E/\partial k^2)$, near the conduction-band minimum and valence-band maximum using the finite-$k$ discrete sampling and numerical differentiation procedures employed in the calculation; the finite $k$-point mesh, energy broadening used in density-of-states computations, and interpolation procedures used in these numerical derivatives are assumed not to bias the curvature estimates beyond the precision of the effective-mass values reported.

Extraction of the density‑of‑states effective mass $m^{*}_{dos}$ for BXGa by fitting the second derivative of the computed band energy $E(\vec{k})$ within a small $k$‑space region around the band edge using the available DFT/mBJ band energies (from the stated $k$‑sampling) yields effective masses suitable for insertion into the deformation‑potential relaxation‑time formula; numerical discretization, modest band nonparabolicity, and interpolation errors are assumed not to change $m^{*}_{dos}$ by more than an order‑unity factor that would qualitatively alter the transport conclusions.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

The extraction-of-effective-mass proposition is that numerical second-derivative estimates of effective mass $m_\alpha$ computed from DFT-LDA band energies $E(k_x,k_y)$ sampled on a finite discrete $k$-point mesh accurately reflect the continuum effective mass relevant to carrier transport, where the numerical definition used is $m_\alpha^{-1}=(1/\hbar^2)\,\partial^2 E/\partial k_\alpha^2$ for direction $\alpha\in\{x,y\}$; this proposition assumes that the sampled $k$-range and resolution, the curvature-fitting procedure applied to the sampled band energies, and the finite-cell periodic boundary conditions do not introduce systematic biases large enough to alter the reported masses by factors comparable to the claimed reductions.

The conduction-band effective mass $m^{*}$ is extracted from the computed TB and DFT band dispersions by numerical evaluation of the second derivative of the energy with respect to crystal momentum via $m^{*}=\hbar^{2}\big/(\partial^{2}E/\partial k^{2})$ evaluated at the conduction-band minimum or at a nearly conical touching, and performing this numerical differentiation on the reported computed bands yields reliable values in the range $3.4\times10^{-3}m_{e}$ to $1.1\times10^{-2}m_{e}$ for the bias regimes studied (here $m_{e}$ is the free-electron mass), within the numerical and fitting tolerances of the presented band-structure data.
